perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will hold the Confidential Information in confidence and will not disclose the Confidential Information to any person or entity without the prior written consent of the Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will endeavor to hold the Confidential Information in confidence and will consider refraining from disclosing the Confidential Information to any person or entity, pending further discussion with the Owner.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "will hold in confidence and will not disclose" with "will endeavor to hold in confidence and will consider refraining...pending further discussion" weakens the obligation and introduces ambiguity. Endeavoring doesn't guarantee confidentiality, and considering refraining leaves room for interpretation and potential disclosure before discussion, conflicting with the strict confidentiality expected.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statute § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 812.081 prohibits the unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to maintain confidentiality, the modified text creates a loophole where a recipient could argue they were only "considering refraining" from disclosure while actively taking steps that could lead to a violation of trade secret laws, contradicting the intent of the statute. The ambiguous wording makes it more difficult to prove the intent necessary for a violation under this statute.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Trade secrets; theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying; definitions; penalty. — (1) As used in this section: (a) “Article” means any object, device, machine, material, substance, or composition of matter, or any mixture or copy thereof, whether in whole or in part, including any complete or partial writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, map, or copy thereof. (b) “Representing” means completely or partially describing, depicting, embodying, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (c) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. “Trade secret” includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a clear obligation on the Recipient to maintain confidentiality. The changed text weakens this obligation by replacing "will hold in confidence and will not disclose" with "will endeavor to hold in confidence and will consider refraining...pending further discussion." This introduces ambiguity and creates a loophole where the recipient could argue they were only 'considering refraining' from disclosure. According to Florida Statute § 812.081, trade secrets must be protected to provide a business advantage. By weakening the confidentiality obligation, the modified text directly undermines the protection of trade secrets, potentially depriving the owner of control, which contradicts the statute's intent as described in the provided law explanation, where the statute directly addresses actions taken 'with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret'.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Owner, the Recipient shall return to the Owner all written materials containing the Confidential Information. The Recipient shall also deliver to the Owner written statements signed by the Recipient certifying that all materials have been returned within five (5) days of receipt of the request.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the written request of the Owner, the Recipient should, where feasible, return to the Owner most of the written materials believed to contain Confidential Information. The Recipient may deliver to the Owner a statement regarding materials returned within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates the return of *all* written materials within five days. The revised text introduces several ambiguities: "should where feasible" makes the return discretionary. "Most of the written materials believed to contain" implies the Recipient can decide what constitutes confidential information and potentially withhold materials. Replacing "shall deliver a signed statement" with "may deliver a statement" makes certification optional, and replacing "five (5) days" with "a reasonable timeframe" introduces significant vagueness.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Uniform Trade Secrets Act, Florida Statute § 688.003
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute § 688.003 allows for injunctive relief to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the return of confidential materials discretionary and the certification optional, the modified text weakens the Owner's ability to ensure the return of all confidential materials and hinders their ability to obtain an injunction if misappropriation is suspected. The statute depends on clear obligations for the protection of trade secrets, which this change undermines.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title XXXIX > Chapter 688 > Section 003 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title XXXIX COMMERCIAL RELATIONS Chapter 688 UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT Entire Chapter SECTION 003 Injunctive relief. F.S. 688.003 688.003 Injunctive relief. — (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. History. — s. 3, ch. 88-254.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces ambiguities that weaken the owner's ability to protect their trade secrets, potentially contradicting Florida Statute § 688.003. The original contract mandated the return of all confidential information, while the changed text makes this discretionary by using the phrase 'should, where feasible'. Furthermore, the original text required a signed statement certifying the return of materials, while the changed text makes this optional by stating the recipient 'may deliver a statement'. This undermines the injunctive relief available under F.S. 688.003, which is designed to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. The original text ensures clear obligations for protecting trade secrets, whereas the modifications weaken these obligations and potentially hinder the owner's ability to obtain an injunction if misappropriation is suspected. The change is explicit and impactful because it directly diminishes the legal protections previously afforded by the contract regarding the return of confidential information.
